title: Textual #5380 — Mouse input stops after VS Code reload
subtitle: OSS issue deep-dive, synthesized into a living doc
scope: Textualize/textual issue #5380 and its orbiting archaeology
sourceCoverage: Issue body and comments, inspect_ai workaround commit, closed related issues #5547 / #1171 / #1031 / #5894, linux_driver.py source
objective: Make Textual's mouse input survive a VS Code 'Developer: Reload Window' without requiring the user to restart the app, and decide whether the fix should ship inside Textual or stay as a per-app workaround.

objectiveFacets.name: Root cause
objectiveFacets.description: Synchronization gap between Textual's one-shot mouse-enable ANSI codes and VS Code's xterm.js, which re-initializes without those codes after a reload.

objectiveFacets.name: Proven workaround
objectiveFacets.description: A production-shipped fix in UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai: override _watch_app_focus and re-call _enable_mouse_support() when the app regains focus.

objectiveFacets.name: Shipping path into Textual
objectiveFacets.description: Open PR that ports the inspect_ai pattern into Textual's own App or driver, behind a flag if needed.

objectiveFacets.name: Cross-terminal idempotency
objectiveFacets.description: Claim that re-enabling mouse ANSI codes is safe must hold for tmux, alacritty, ghostty, kitty, Windows Terminal — not only xterm.js.

invariants.name: Mouse-enable ANSI codes are idempotent
invariants.statement: Writing the four mouse-enable ANSI codes again when they are already active is a no-op on xterm.js; assertion has not been independently verified on other terminals.

invariants.name: Mouse is enabled once, never refreshed
invariants.statement: Textual's Linux driver calls _enable_mouse_support() once inside start_application_mode and has no resend path during the app's running lifetime.

sections.title: Findings
sections.rationale: These are observed facts recovered from the issue thread — not decisions, not implementation tasks. Each item traces back to a specific comment, diagnostic, or commit.

data.name: VS Code reload drops mouse tracking silently

notes.text: Running any Textual app in the VS Code integrated terminal, then triggering `Developer: Reload Window`, leaves the app alive with working keyboard input but no mouse events. The app has no signal that reload happened.
Reproduces on Linux (Ubuntu 22.04, VS Code 1.95.3, Textual 0.89.1) per the issue body.

data.name: tmux session inside VS Code is immune

notes.text: Running the same Textual app under tmux inside VS Code survives the reload. tmux re-asserts terminal state on redraw; VS Code reload does not inherit that behaviour.
Attribution: jjallaire-aisi, 2024-12-11 comment.
`with self.app.suspend(): ...` fully restores mouse input after the block exits, because resume re-runs start_application_mode and re-sends the ANSI codes.
Confirmed the cause is in the mouse-enable path, not in xterm.js's state tracking more broadly.
Inline comment on the inspect_ai workaround: 'ansi codes for enabling mouse support are idempotent so it is fine to do this even in cases where mouse support is already enabled.'
Assertion has only been validated in practice on xterm.js (VS Code). Behaviour on alacritty, kitty, ghostty, Windows Terminal, mintty is untested in this thread.

sections.title: Current mouse-enable flow in Textual
sections.rationale: This is a capability (what the code currently does in the running app), not a finding. Separating the code behaviour from the observations keeps the diagnostic chain clean.

notes.text: src/textual/drivers/linux_driver.py:248 — the only call site inside start_application_mode. No re-call path during running lifetime.

data.name: Four ANSI codes written on enable

notes.text: linux_driver.py:121–133 writes: \x1b[?1000h (VT200 mouse), \x1b[?1003h (any-event mouse), \x1b[?1015h (urxvt mode, deprecated), \x1b[?1006h (SGR extended — the format xterm.js actually uses), then flush().

linux_driver.py:169 writes the `?…l` form of each code. Called during clean exit — this is also why issue #1171 (coordinates leaking to shell prompt after force-kill) is a symmetric twin: disable never runs.

Textual's App has a _watch_app_focus(focus: bool) method that fires when the app gains or loses focus. VS Code reload → app regains focus → hook fires. This is the insertion point the inspect_ai workaround uses.

sections.title: Attempted fixes and workarounds
sections.rationale: Each attempt is a factual record of what was tried and what it revealed — not a decision. The rejected and shipped attempts both carry the same investigative weight.

notes.text: Confirmed the root mechanism (re-running start_application_mode restores mouse) but is not a production fix — requires the user to invoke suspend() manually and yanks the whole screen.
Calling driver._enable_mouse_support() alone restores the mouse, isolating the cause from the rest of start_application_mode. This narrowed the production fix.

UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai commit 8ce3409: overrides _watch_app_focus on the App subclass; when focus becomes True, calls the driver's _enable_mouse_support() wrapped in try/except. ~20 LOC including a helper. Ships in production.

Pattern is cleanly portable into Textual itself: the App base class could call _enable_mouse_support() on focus-regain by default, possibly behind a config flag for terminals where idempotency is unverified.

sections.title: Related archaeology
sections.rationale: Prior issues in the same problem space. Each is a factual record of adjacent or symmetric bugs — they tell a contributor where the repo has already walked and what was learned.

data.name: #5547 — Mouse broken in VS Code (smooth-scroll regression, CLOSED)

notes.text: Different root cause — mouse coordinates mis-calculated after commit 9f18dfe (smooth scrolling). Closed by PRs #5549 and #5551. Shares the 'VS Code + Textual mouse' surface but is not the same bug as #5380.

data.name: #1171 — Symmetric leak: coords appear at shell prompt after force-stop (CLOSED)

notes.text: When the VS Code debugger force-stops a Textual app, _disable_mouse_support() never runs, so xterm.js keeps emitting mouse coordinates into the next shell prompt. Same root-cause family as #5380: state drift between Textual's ANSI codes and xterm.js. Closed without an explicit linked PR.

data.name: #1031 — Mouse not working in VS Code Windows terminal (CLOSED, 2022)

notes.text: Older Windows-specific mouse issue in the VS Code terminal. Likely resolved by intervening changes and does not share the reload-cycle root cause.
Adjacent surface. Worth re-reading only if the cross-terminal idempotency check reveals Windows-specific behaviour.

sections.title: Open debate — shipping path
sections.rationale: These are stances and open questions about whether/how to ship the fix — clearly decisions, not findings or code facts. The section preserves the maintainer/user exchange so the next contributor doesn't re-litigate from scratch.

notes.text: willmcgugan, 2024-12-11: 'I don't think this is something we can fix. I think you should raise it on the VSCode repository.' No follow-up after the user's pushback and workaround.
jjallaire-aisi argued: VS Code bugs of this kind 'die on the vine'; Textual has stronger incentive and technical basis; and the fix is small and already validated in inspect_ai.

As of 2026-04-18 the issue is OPEN with zero linked PRs and no labels. The technical blocker is not knowledge — the pattern is proven — it is maintainer stance plus an unowned cross-terminal risk check.

data.name: Open question: gate the fix, or apply always?

notes.text: If idempotency holds on every terminal Textual supports, the fix can be unconditional. If not, it needs to be opt-in (config flag) or gated on detected VS Code environment (TERM_PROGRAM=vscode). No one has run this check yet.
